sources.title: Statutes
sources.tagline: The written laws enacted by elected legislators.
sources.madeBy: A legislature — the elected representatives of the public.
sources.role: Sets the broad, binding rules and policy choices for a jurisdiction.
sources.whenUseful: When you need the foundational, democratically enacted rule on a subject. Statutes carry broad authority and can create entirely new rights, duties, programs, or crimes.

workflow.label: Bill
workflow.brief: A proposed law introduced by a legislator. It has been written down and formally offered, but it is not yet law and has no legal effect.
workflow.use: The starting point: an idea for a new rule that must survive committee review, debate, and votes before it can bind anyone.

workflow.label: Session Law
workflow.brief: Once the legislature passes the bill and the executive signs it (or a veto is overridden), it becomes an enacted law, published in the order in which it was passed.
workflow.use: The authoritative record of exactly what the legislature adopted, and when — the law as enacted, before it is reorganized.

workflow.label: Codified Statute
workflow.brief: The enacted law is broken apart and filed by subject into the jurisdiction's code, with later amendments woven in alongside related laws.
workflow.use: Where lawyers and the public actually find the current law on a topic. Codification gathers scattered enactments into one organized, up-to-date place.

deepDive.intro: A statute is a law written and enacted by a legislature — the branch made up of elected representatives. In most modern legal systems, statutes are the primary form of law, because they come directly from the people's chosen lawmakers.

sections.heading: What a statute is
sections.body: A statute is a binding rule adopted by the legislature. Because it comes from elected lawmakers, it is usually the most direct expression of public policy, and it can do things no other source can do on its own: create new programs, define crimes, grant or remove rights, and raise and spend public money.

sections.heading: How a statute is made
sections.body: A statute begins life as a bill — a proposed law that any legislator can introduce. The bill is studied in committee, debated, amended, and voted on. To become law it must pass the legislature and then be signed by the executive (or, if vetoed, be re-passed by a large enough majority). At that moment it becomes a session law. Later, it is codified: editors place it by subject into the jurisdiction's code so it can be found and read alongside related laws.

sections.heading: Session law versus codified statute
sections.body: These are the same law in two different arrangements. The session law is the official text exactly as enacted, frozen in time and numbered in the order it passed — useful when you need to see precisely what the law said at a given moment. The codified statute is that same law reorganized by topic and kept current as amendments are added — useful when you simply need the law in force today. Knowing both exist explains why a single statute can be found in two very different-looking places.

sections.heading: When a statute is the right source
sections.body: Reach for a statute when you need broad, durable authority: a brand-new rule where none exists, the controlling policy on a subject, or a rule that applies across the whole jurisdiction. As a general matter, statutes outrank regulations and ordinary case law on the same subject, so a statute is also where you look first to see whether those other sources are even allowed to operate.

deepDive.whyUseful: Statutes are the backbone of the legal system: broad in reach, forward-looking, and democratically legitimate. They are generally supreme over regulations and over case law that merely interprets a statute, which makes them the natural place to start when you ask, 'What is the rule, and who is allowed to change it?'

keyTerms.definition: A proposed law that has been introduced but not yet enacted.
The branch of elected representatives that makes statutes.
To formally make a bill into law.
An enacted law published in the order it was passed.
To organize enacted laws by subject into a code.
A formal change to an existing statute.

sources.title: Regulations
sources.tagline: The detailed rules agencies issue to carry out a statute.
sources.madeBy: An executive agency, using authority that a statute delegated to it.
sources.role: Fills in and implements the broad commands of a statute with specific, technical detail.
sources.whenUseful: When you need the precise, operational specifics a statute leaves to experts — and when the legislature has handed the subject to an agency.

workflow.label: Authorizing Statute
workflow.brief: A statute gives an agency the power to make rules on a subject and sets the limits of that power.
workflow.use: Regulations exist only because a statute authorized them. This is both the source of the agency's authority and the leash on it.

workflow.label: Proposed Rule
workflow.brief: The agency publishes a draft rule and invites the public to comment on it during a set period.
workflow.use: Lets affected people respond before a rule binds them, and builds a record the agency must consider before finalizing.

workflow.label: Final Rule
workflow.brief: After reviewing the comments, the agency issues the binding final rule, usually with an explanation of its reasoning.
workflow.use: This is the rule that now carries the force of law. The accompanying explanation lets the public and courts understand and check it.

workflow.label: Codified Regulation
workflow.brief: The final rule is organized by subject into the jurisdiction's regulatory code.
workflow.use: Where you find the current operative regulations on a topic, gathered with related rules.

deepDive.intro: A regulation — sometimes called a 'rule' — is a detailed directive issued by an executive agency to put a statute into practice. Agencies are staffed by subject-matter specialists, so regulations are where broad legislative goals become concrete, workable requirements.

sections.heading: What a regulation is
sections.body: A regulation is a binding rule issued by an executive agency rather than by the legislature. Statutes often set a goal in general terms — 'keep the water safe,' 'prevent unfair trade practices' — and leave the technical specifics to experts. The regulation supplies those specifics: the exact limits, procedures, forms, and definitions.

sections.heading: Where the authority comes from
sections.body: Agencies have no inherent power to make law. A statute must first delegate authority to the agency and mark its boundaries. This matters in practice: a regulation that goes beyond what its authorizing statute allows can be challenged and struck down. Every regulation is therefore tethered to a statute behind it.

sections.heading: How a regulation is made
sections.body: Once a statute grants authority, the agency typically uses a public process. It first issues a proposed rule and invites comments from anyone affected. After reading and weighing those comments, it issues a final rule, usually accompanied by an explanation that responds to the major objections. The final rule is then codified — placed by subject into the regulatory code — so it can be found alongside related rules. The comment step is what gives the public a voice and gives courts a record to review.

sections.heading: When a regulation is the right source
sections.body: Reach for a regulation when you need precise, technical, operational detail; when the subject is one the legislature handed to an agency; or when the rules need to keep pace with changing facts. Statutes change slowly; regulations can be updated far more nimbly within the authority the statute provides.

deepDive.whyUseful: Regulations translate broad statutory goals into rules people can actually follow. They are expert-driven, far quicker to update than statutes, and binding like law — but only within the boundaries their statute sets, which keeps them accountable to the legislature's choices.

keyTerms.definition: An executive body that administers a statute and issues regulations.
The statute that grants and limits an agency's rulemaking power.
A draft regulation published for public comment; not yet binding.
The window during which the public can respond to a proposed rule.
The binding regulation issued after comments are considered.
A final rule organized by subject into the regulatory code.

sources.title: Cases
sources.tagline: Courts deciding real disputes — and, in the process, making precedent.
sources.madeBy: Courts (judges), deciding actual disputes between parties.
sources.role: Resolves a specific dispute and, when published, interprets the law and creates precedent that guides later courts.
sources.whenUseful: When you need to know how the law applies to facts, what ambiguous statutory or regulatory language means, or what the rule is where no statute speaks.

workflow.label: Appellate Dispute
workflow.brief: A party who lost in the trial court asks a higher court to review legal errors. The appellate court focuses on questions of law, not on re-trying the facts.
workflow.use: Appeals are where courts typically announce rules that will guide future cases — the engine of precedent. (The dispute first passed through a trial court below.)

workflow.label: Slip Opinion
workflow.brief: The court's written decision as first released, on its own, right after the court rules — before official publication and subject to later correction.
workflow.use: The earliest official statement of the court's reasoning and result; how the parties and public first learn the outcome.

workflow.label: Published Opinion
workflow.brief: The finalized decision printed in the official reports, fixed in form and citable.
workflow.use: Once published, the opinion is precedent — later courts look to it to decide similar cases.

deepDive.intro: A case is a court's written decision resolving a real dispute between parties. Along the way, the court must say what the law is and how it applies. When that decision is published, its reasoning guides future courts — and that is how cases make law through precedent.

sections.heading: What a case is
sections.body: A court decides an actual controversy between parties, and in doing so it interprets and applies the law to a concrete set of facts. The written explanation of that decision is the opinion. Unlike a statute, a case does not announce a rule in the abstract; it shows the law in action, settling exactly how a rule plays out when real facts meet it.

sections.heading: Why appeals matter most for law-making
sections.body: Trial courts mostly resolve who wins on the facts of a single dispute, and their decisions usually do not bind anyone else. Appellate courts focus on questions of law, and their published opinions are what create precedent for future cases. That is why this module follows a dispute at the appellate stage: it is where a single case turns into guidance for everyone.

sections.heading: How a precedent is made
sections.body: After an appellate court decides, it releases a slip opinion — the decision standing alone, issued right away and still open to correction. Once finalized, it becomes a published opinion in the official reports: fixed in form, assigned a citation, and available for any later court or lawyer to rely on. Publication is the moment a one-off decision becomes durable law.

sections.heading: When a case is the right source
sections.body: Reach for a case when you need to know how the law actually applies: what an ambiguous statute or regulation means in practice, how a rule resolves a specific fact pattern, or what the governing rule is where no statute addresses the issue at all. In that last situation, the court itself supplies the rule — law made entirely through cases.

deepDive.whyUseful: Cases give law its meaning in the real world. They resolve ambiguity, adapt rules to new facts, and fill gaps where no statute speaks. Through precedent, they also make the law predictable: once a court has decided how a rule applies, others can rely on that answer.

keyTerms.definition: The body and official that decide disputes and write opinions.
A request that a higher court review a lower court's decision for legal error.
An issue about what the law is or means, as opposed to what the facts were.
A court's decision as first released, before official publication and subject to correction.
The finalized, citable decision printed in the official reports.
A published decision that guides how later courts decide similar cases.
